So this is a right muscle.
I'm using a monopolar scissors for the dissection and a Cartier grasper, which is an atraumatic grasper.
It's best to initiate the dissection in the submuscular plane.
If you dissect the muscle off of the subcutaneous tissue first, the insufflation will flatten the muscle against the chest.
Now I'm going to dissect the superficial surface.
So I'm pulling the muscle down and toward the camera, and I'm dissecting the subcutaneous tissue off of it.
Every time you see a little blue camera flash on screen, I'm using the hand controls to move the camera instead of the arms.
Every time you see the two hands come up, I'm clutching.
This means I'm moving the controls independently of the arms.
This is important and allows the surgeon to achieve a more natural position without moving the instruments themselves.
I'm working back and forth along the entire leading edge of the dissection to maintain a good exposure, and I'm allowing insufflation to do the rest.
Because this is a large working area, the instruments can come into conflict outside the body as they line up at the extremes of dissection.
This should be actively managed.

This was probably overly cautious, and I have not clipped the vessel in the last 20 cases.

This was to protect the pedicle.
Now, once you have the whole superficial and deep surfaces dissected, you can detach the muscle.
Take the monopolar scissors and simply divide the inferior posterior attachment.

You can see how clean this dissection is.
There's barely any muscle left behind.
Once you get up to the level of the scapular tip, you're essentially done.
